serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of Yu Sheng at a restaurant or home gathering is approximately 200 g, representing one person's share of a communal tossed salad platter.

The input record's per_100g values are accepted as the HPB canonical values for this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Salad Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database: Mixed Vegetable Salad with Dressing and Refined Carbohydrate Components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Yu Sheng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed salad dishes with plum sauce and crispy cracker components. The dish contains raw vegetables (low GI), raw fish (negligible GI contribution), and a moderate amount of refined carbohydrate from crackers and plum sauce. The composite GI is estimated conservatively at 45. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 45.22) / 100 ≈ 20.4, using a 200 g serving with 22.61 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (562 mg per 100 g; approximately 1124 mg per 200 g serving, representing ~49% of the 2300 mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Yu Sheng portion size and request reduced plum sauce and soy-based condiments, as a single 200 g serving can contribute nearly half the recommended daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (22.61 g per 100 g) with low estimated GI (~45), but plum sauce and crispy crackers contribute rapidly absorbed sugars and refined starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Yu Sheng in moderated portions (100–150 g), prioritising the raw fish and vegetable components while minimising the plum sauce and crispy crackers to reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (562 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; however, raw fish provides omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, Yu Sheng should be consumed in small portions with reduced sauce to limit sodium intake, while the omega-3-rich raw fish component offers a cardioprotective benefit when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yu Sheng (鱼生), also known as Yusheng, Lo Hei, or Prosperity Raw Fish Salad, is a iconic Singaporean Chinese New Year dish traditionally consumed during the Lunar New Year festive season. The dish consists of thinly sliced raw fish — most commonly salmon or yellowtail — arranged over a colourful bed of finely shredded vegetables including white radish, carrot, cucumber, and pickled ginger, garnished with pomelo segments, crispy fried crackers, sesame seeds, and five-spice powder. The entire platter is dressed with a sweet-sour plum sauce and sesame oil just before serving. The communal ritual of 'Lo Hei' (捞起) involves all diners tossing the salad together with chopsticks while calling out auspicious phrases, symbolising prosperity, good luck, and abundance for the new year. Nutritionally, Yu Sheng provides moderate protein from the raw fish (14.73 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrates from the sauce and crackers (22.61 g per 100 g), and a notable sodium content (562 mg per 100 g) primarily from the plum sauce and condiments. The dish is rich in calcium (188 mg per 100 g) and provides iron (1.76 mg per 100 g). The estimated glycaemic index is low (~45), though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The raw fish component contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids. Yu Sheng is not suitable for individuals with fish allergies or those requiring halal-certified food unless specifically prepared with halal-certified ingredients.

notes: CRITICAL DATA QUALITY NOTE: The input record cr_id G00011043 was named '50X Disposable Chopsticks For Yu Sheng (Individually Wrapped)' — a non-food utensil item. However, the nutrition data (energy 262 kcal, protein 14.73 g, fat 12.44 g, carbohydrate 22.61 g, sodium 562 mg) is clearly that of a food item, not chopsticks. The record description explicitly references 'cny yusheng' and the food_group is 'Mixed / Customisable mixed dishes'. The HPB data has been correctly identified as pertaining to Yu Sheng (Prosperity Raw Fish Salad), and the food name has been corrected accordingly. The original input name was a data entry error — the chopsticks product name was incorrectly associated with the Yu Sheng nutrition record.
